reasoning_model_1: The user's command explicitly requested the text to be read 'with hiccups', a form of non-linguistic vocalization. Model 1 completely failed to adhere to this paralinguistic instruction. Instead of simulating the sound of a hiccup or acting out the condition, the model literally pronounced the word 'Hiccup' as a linguistic term (/ˈhɪkʌp/) at 00:00. Throughout the remainder of the audio (00:01–00:12), the speech is fluent and clear but entirely devoid of any hiccup sounds or interrupted rhythms that would suggest the speaker is hiccuping. Consequently, the model failed to produce the target feature entirely.
reasoning_model_2: Model 2 attempted to follow the instruction by generating a non-linguistic sound at 00:00 in place of the text 'Hiccup!'. The sound produced is a short, breathy vocalization (/hə/). While this demonstrates an ability to control non-linguistic features, the quality of the sound is ambiguous and resembles a short chuckle or laugh more than a distinct hiccup. Furthermore, the model failed to sustain the requested style; the instruction implied reading the sentence *with* hiccups (plural), and the text mentions 'can't stop hiccuping', yet the rest of the speech (00:01–00:14) is delivered with normal fluency and no further interruptions. Thus, the feature is present but insufficiently expressed and lacks consistency.
model_comparison: Model 2 wins because it successfully generated a non-linguistic vocalization, attempting to interpret the prompt 'Hiccup!' as a sound effect rather than text. Although the sound was acoustically imperfect (sounding slightly like a laugh) and the feature was not maintained throughout the sentence, it demonstrated partial compliance with the 'Non-Linguistic Vocalizations' category. In contrast, Model 1 scored a 0 because it treated the prompt purely as a text-to-speech task, reading the onomatopoeia as a spoken word and ignoring the instruction to simulate the physiological act of hiccuping.
